news_summary: Today's positive human rights news highlights progress in gender equality as vital for national security, the importance of feminist leadership in strengthening democracy, and Germany's efforts to address menopause openly in the workplace.
news_summary_fr: Les bonnes nouvelles du jour en matière de droits humains mettent en avant les progrès réalisés en matière d'égalité des sexes, essentiels pour la sécurité nationale, l'importance du leadership féministe dans le renforcement de la démocratie et les efforts déployés par l'Allemagne pour aborder ouvertement la ménopause sur le lieu de travail.
news_summary_es: Las noticias positivas de hoy sobre derechos humanos destacan los avances en materia de igualdad de género como elemento fundamental para la seguridad nacional, la importancia del liderazgo feminista en el fortalecimiento de la democracia y los esfuerzos de Alemania por abordar abiertamente la menopausia en el lugar de trabajo.

articles.title: Australia: Gender Equality Essential for National Security
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Australia Minister for Foreign Affairs Penny Wong addresses the 78th session of the United Nations General Assembly at United Nations headquarters, September 22, 2023. 
                    © 2023 Craig Ruttle/AP Photo
          
    


  
(Sydney) – The Australian government should strengthen gender-focused approaches across all crisis responses, Human Rights Watch said today following International Women’s Day. The authorities should acknowledge that gender equality is essential to global peace, security, and justice by supporting women-led organizations and ensuring that women are meaningfully included in decision making.Human Rights Watch in February 2026 made a submission to the Joint Standing Committee on Foreign Affairs, Defense and Trade inquiry addressing gender equality as a national security and economic security imperative. Human Rights Watch highlighted the gender-related impact of conflict, crisis, and climate change; the dangerous erosion of the basic principles of the United Nations Security Council Resolution 1325 on Women, Peace and Security; and the need to push for a gender-sensitive UN crimes against humanity treaty.“International Women’s Day is a key opportunity for the Australian government to be thinking about gender equality, both domestically and abroad,” said Daniela Gavshon, Australia director at Human Rights Watch. “Human Rights Watch’s submission to the Joint Standing Committee emphasizes that undermining gender equality violates international human rights law, fuels instability, and weakens countries’ resilience to crises.”Amid rising militarism, attacks on women human rights defenders, and efforts to roll back gender rights across the globe, Australia’s leadership in advancing and defending the Women, Peace and Security agenda is paramount, Human Rights Watch said.The Australian government should support the development of an international treaty on crimes against humanity, which provides a crucial opportunity to strengthen international criminal law protections for women and girls, and support such as creating the crime of gender apartheid. Australia should expand asylum pathways for women and girls fleeing persecution and adopt a feminist foreign policy that places the rights of women and girls at the center of all foreign policy, defense, and national security decisions, Human Rights Watch said.“Australia has the tools to effectively promote women’s rights around the world, including a foreign minister committed to gender equality and having an ambassador for gender equality,” Gavshon said. “The Australian government can become a global leader on women’s and girls’ rights by making them a more prominent part of its foreign policy.”
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Australia Minister for Foreign Affairs Penny Wong addresses the 78th session of the United Nations General Assembly at United Nations headquarters, September 22, 2023. © 2023 Craig Ruttle/AP Photo

(Sydney) – The Australian government should strengthen gender-focused approaches across all crisis responses, Human Rights Watch said today following International Women’s Day. The authorities should acknowledge that gender equality is essential to global peace, security, and justice by supporting women-led organizations and ensuring that women are meaningfully included in decision making.

Human Rights Watch in February 2026 made a submission to the Joint Standing Committee on Foreign Affairs, Defense and Trade inquiry addressing gender equality as a national security and economic security imperative. Human Rights Watch highlighted the gender-related impact of conflict, crisis, and climate change; the dangerous erosion of the basic principles of the United Nations Security Council Resolution 1325 on Women, Peace and Security; and the need to push for a gender-sensitive UN crimes against humanity treaty.

“International Women’s Day is a key opportunity for the Australian government to be thinking about gender equality, both domestically and abroad,” said Daniela Gavshon, Australia director at Human Rights Watch. “Human Rights Watch’s submission to the Joint Standing Committee emphasizes that undermining gender equality violates international human rights law, fuels instability, and weakens countries’ resilience to crises.”

Amid rising militarism, attacks on women human rights defenders, and efforts to roll back gender rights across the globe, Australia’s leadership in advancing and defending the Women, Peace and Security agenda is paramount, Human Rights Watch said.

The Australian government should support the development of an international treaty on crimes against humanity, which provides a crucial opportunity to strengthen international criminal law protections for women and girls, and support such as creating the crime of gender apartheid. Australia should expand asylum pathways for women and girls fleeing persecution and adopt a feminist foreign policy that places the rights of women and girls at the center of all foreign policy, defense, and national security decisions, Human Rights Watch said.

“Australia has the tools to effectively promote women’s rights around the world, including a foreign minister committed to gender equality and having an ambassador for gender equality,” Gavshon said. “The Australian government can become a global leader on women’s and girls’ rights by making them a more prominent part of its foreign policy.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights Australia’s potential leadership in advancing gender equality as a critical component of national and global security, emphasizing support for women-led organizations, gender-sensitive policies, and international treaties. This represents a meaningful and substantive positive impact on human rights with broad societal implications.
articles.personality_title: Australia urged to make gender equality a national security priority
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Human Rights Watch has called on the Australian government to include gender equality in all crisis and security plans. This follows International Women’s Day and a recent report to a government committee about how important women’s rights are for peace and safety.

**What happened** – In February 2026, Human Rights Watch submitted advice to Australia’s Joint Standing Committee on Foreign Affairs, Defense and Trade. They explained how conflicts, climate change, and crises affect women differently. They also warned that key UN rules about women’s role in peace and security are being ignored. They urged Australia to support a new UN treaty to better protect women against serious crimes.

**Impact** – This is important because ignoring gender equality can make conflicts worse and make countries less able to handle crises. Human Rights Watch says Australia can lead the world by supporting women’s rights in its foreign policy and security decisions. This includes helping women-led groups and making sure women have a real say in important choices.

**What’s next step** – Australia is encouraged to back the new UN treaty on crimes against humanity with a focus on gender issues. The country should also offer more help to women and girls escaping danger and make women’s rights a central part of its foreign and security policies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Human Rights Watch wants Australia to treat gender equality as a key part of national security to build safer, fairer communities at home and abroad.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'Australie invitée à faire de l'égalité des sexes une priorité de sécurité nationale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Human Rights Watch a demandé au gouvernement australien d’intégrer l’égalité des sexes dans toutes les réponses aux crises et la sécurité. Cela fait suite à la Journée internationale des femmes et à un rapport récent soumis à un comité gouvernemental sur l’importance des droits des femmes pour la paix et la sécurité.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En février 2026, Human Rights Watch a soumis des recommandations au Comité permanent mixte des affaires étrangères, de la défense et du commerce de l’Australie. Ils ont expliqué comment les conflits, le changement climatique et les crises affectent différemment les femmes. Ils ont aussi alerté sur le non-respect des règles clés de l’ONU concernant le rôle des femmes dans la paix et la sécurité. Ils ont encouragé l’Australie à soutenir un nouveau traité de l’ONU pour mieux protéger les femmes contre les crimes graves.

**Impact** – C’est important car ignorer l’égalité des sexes peut aggraver les conflits et rendre les pays moins capables de gérer les crises. Human Rights Watch affirme que l’Australie peut devenir un leader mondial en soutenant les droits des femmes dans ses politiques étrangères et de sécurité. Cela inclut le soutien aux organisations dirigées par des femmes et l’assurance que les femmes participent vraiment aux décisions importantes.

**Prochaine étape** – L’Australie est encouragée à soutenir le nouveau traité de l’ONU sur les crimes contre l’humanité, en mettant l’accent sur les questions de genre. Le pays devrait aussi offrir plus d’aide aux femmes et filles fuyant le danger et faire des droits des femmes une priorité dans ses politiques étrangères et de sécurité.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Human Rights Watch souhaite que l’Australie considère l’égalité des sexes comme un élément clé de la sécurité nationale pour construire des communautés plus sûres et plus justes, ici et dans le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: Australia instada a hacer de la igualdad de género una prioridad de seguridad nacional
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Human Rights Watch ha pedido al gobierno australiano que incluya la igualdad de género en todas las respuestas a crisis y en la seguridad. Esto sigue al Día Internacional de la Mujer y a un informe reciente presentado a un comité gubernamental sobre la importancia de los derechos de las mujeres para la paz y la seguridad.

**Qué pasó** – En febrero de 2026, Human Rights Watch presentó recomendaciones al Comité Permanente Conjunto de Asuntos Exteriores, Defensa y Comercio de Australia. Explicaron cómo los conflictos, el cambio climático y las crisis afectan de manera diferente a las mujeres. También advirtieron que se están ignorando reglas clave de la ONU sobre el papel de las mujeres en la paz y la seguridad. Instaron a Australia a apoyar un nuevo tratado de la ONU para proteger mejor a las mujeres contra crímenes graves.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque ignorar la igualdad de género puede empeorar los conflictos y hacer que los países sean menos capaces de manejar crisis. Human Rights Watch dice que Australia puede liderar el mundo apoyando los derechos de las mujeres en sus políticas exteriores y de seguridad. Esto incluye apoyar a organizaciones dirigidas por mujeres y asegurar que las mujeres tengan una voz real en decisiones importantes.

**Próximo paso** – Se anima a Australia a apoyar el nuevo tratado de la ONU sobre crímenes de lesa humanidad con enfoque en temas de género. El país también debería ofrecer más ayuda a mujeres y niñas que huyen del peligro y hacer de los derechos de las mujeres una parte central de sus políticas exteriores y de seguridad.

**Resumen en una frase** – Human Rights Watch quiere que Australia trate la igualdad de género como una parte clave de la seguridad nacional para construir comunidades más seguras y justas, tanto en casa como en el extranjero.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a diverse group of women-shaped silhouettes standing together on a globe, their arms linked in unity, surrounded by delicate scales of justice balanced evenly, with soft, natural earth tones and gentle light symbolizing peace, equality, and global security.

articles.title: Democracy needs women: Feminist leadership in times of shrinking enabling environments for civil society
articles.summary: When feminist leadership expands, democracy deepens. It becomes more accountable, inclusive, and participatory. Women’s civic participation is not about political correctness but about democratic survival.
articles.content:
By Clarisse Sih and Bibbi Abruzzini

At a time when democratic backsliding is no longer an abstract warning but a lived reality across continents, feminist leaders are quietly — and often at personal risk — holding the line.

From election monitoring in Tanzania to newsroom reform in Cameroon, from challenging toxic masculinities to confronting the power of digital platforms, women and feminist allies are defending the enabling environment for civil society in ways that reveal a critical truth: democracy is not gender-neutral, and when women’s participation is restricted, democracy itself weakens.

An enabling environment for civil society is the foundation, not a footnote

In Tanzania, feminist advocate Martina Kabisama has spent years working at the intersection of women’s political participation and social protection. For her, the link between democracy and gender justice is structural. “You cannot advance gender justice where civic space is restricted,” she argues.

Kabisama’s work underscores a reality often overlooked in global policy debates: women’s political participation does not begin at the ballot box. It begins with safety, economic security, and the ability to organize.

When women lack access to social protection systems — income support, legal protections, basic services — they are effectively excluded from civic life. Economic precarity limits mobility. It silences dissent. It narrows participation to those who can afford it.

In contexts where the enabling environment for civil society is shrinking, whether — through restrictive laws, surveillance, or informal intimidation, — women activists are often the first to feel the pressure. In China, members of The Feminist Five were detained in 2015 simply for planning a public campaign against sexual harassment on public transport, a move widely seen as an attempt to silence feminist organizing.

Similarly, Moroccan blogger and human rights defender Saida El Alami has faced repeated arrests linked to her online criticism of authorities and advocacy for political detainees. Online spaces can also become sites of coordinated attacks: Brazilian journalist Patrícia Campos Mello faced a major harassment campaign after reporting on election disinformation, including threats and sexualised smear campaigns amplified by political actors.

Across parts of East Africa, women have taken leading roles in election observation, community mediation, and civic education — not as symbolic participants, but as architects of democratic accountability.

For Kabisama Martina, feminist leadership is not about representation alone. It is about transforming power structures so that democracy works for those historically excluded from it.

Media narratives and the politics of masculinity

In Cameroon, journalist and media executive Beau-Bernard Fonka Mutta approaches democracy from another angle: the cultural narratives that shape who is seen as legitimate in public life.

Raised in an environment where boys were taught not to cry, not to show vulnerability, and to equate masculinity with dominance, Mutta reflects critically on how these norms spill into politics and media. “Society imposes on us what a man should be,” he says. You should not show emotion. You should be strong. Brave. Dominant.”

These expectations do not remain confined to private life. They influence leadership styles, political discourse, and even newsroom cultures.

“I remember a top executive at our news agency saying that whenever he wants to discuss serious issues, he makes sure just men are at the table because women are not intelligent. Their job, according to him, is to be pretty and on air; the brainstorming is reserved for men,” Beau-Bernard recounts. “I remember asking myself where I was and with what sort of people I was dealing with because I know many super intelligent women.”

When dominance is normalized as strength, dialogue becomes weakness. When aggression is coded as authority, democratic debate narrows.

MuttaBeau-Bernard identifies as a feminist African man — a position that challenges the idea that gender justice is a “women’s issue.” For him, healthy masculinity means rejecting violence, embracing emotional literacy, and supporting women’s leadership not as a concession but as a democratic necessity.

The media plays a decisive role here. Newsrooms can either reproduce harmful stereotypes — portraying women as secondary, emotional, or unfit for leadership — or actively dismantle them.

Journalism, Mutta argues, must interrogate the narratives it amplifies. Because the media does not simply report on democracy, it shapes the conditions under which democracy functions.

Digital power and democratic risk

If traditional civic space is shrinking, digital space offers both opportunity and new danger.

Cameroonian journalist and media leader Evelyn Mengue A Koung, recently appointed the first woman and youngest Central Director for Television at the country’s national broadcaster, sees the digital era as double-edged.

On one hand, social media platforms allow women, even those in remote villages, to bypass traditional gatekeepers and tell their own stories. Digital tools can amplify marginalized voices, create networks of solidarity, and put local struggles on the international scene.

“From your smartphone, you can make yourself known to the world,” Kounge explains.

But the same platforms can quickly and easily become tools of silencing.

Cyberharassment, coordinated disinformation campaigns, and algorithmic bias disproportionately target women in public life. A single false rumour can take years to repair. Online abuse pushes women out of political and media spaces — effectively shrinking democratic participation through digital violence.

Koung also raises concerns about agenda-setting power. Tech giants and content aggregators increasingly determine what is visible, what trends, and what disappears.

In this environment, democratic discourse can be distorted — not by overt censorship, but by attention economies that privilege sensationalism over substance.

According to her, public interest media must reclaim its ethical responsibility: to elevate overlooked social issues, to protect marginalized voices, and to resist becoming passive conduits for algorithm-driven narratives.

Digital governance, then, is not just a tech issue — it is a democratic one.

Democracy is not gender neutral

Taken together, these stories reveal a shared pattern: feminists are not only participating in democracy, but they are also sustaining it.

They are monitoring elections when trust erodes. They are advocating for social protection systems that enable civic participation. They are reforming media institutions from within. They are confronting toxic gender norms that normalize domination over dialogue. They are challenging online violence that seeks to silence them.

Yet their work unfolds in increasingly hostile environments: shrinking enabling environments, rising authoritarian tendencies, digital repression, and cultural backlash.

The erosion of women’s rights to organize, speak, and lead is not collateral damage. It is an early warning sign of democratic decline. When women are pushed out of public life, – whether through legal restrictions, economic exclusion, media stereotypes or online harassment, – democratic institutions lose legitimacy and resilience.

Conversely, when feminist leadership expands, democracy deepens. It becomes more accountable, inclusive, and participatory. Women’s civic participation is not about political correctness but about democratic survival.

As these leaders demonstrate, democracy does not defend itself. Women are defending it — in courtrooms, in classrooms, in newsrooms, in digital spaces, and in communities.

The question is whether institutions will meet them with protection, resources and recognition, — or continue to treat their work as peripheral. In times of shrinking civic space, one truth remains constant: without women, democracy erodes — both offline and online.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the critical role of feminist leadership in defending and deepening democracy globally, emphasizing women's civic participation as essential for democratic survival. It details real-world actions by women activists across multiple countries, addressing structural barriers, media reform, and digital challenges. This represents a significant positive impact on democratic accountability and inclusion, with broad societal relevance and substantial context.
articles.personality_title: Women’s leadership strengthens democracy amid growing challenges
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Around the world, democracy is facing serious threats. In many places, laws and social pressures are making it harder for people, especially women, to take part in public life and decision-making.

**What happened** – Women leaders and feminist activists are stepping up to protect democracy. In countries like Tanzania, Cameroon, and Brazil, they work on election monitoring, changing how news is reported, and fighting harmful ideas about gender. They also face risks such as harassment and legal troubles but continue their efforts to make sure everyone can participate fairly.

**Impact** – This is important because democracy depends on people having equal chances to speak and take part. When women are excluded by unfair rules, violence, or online attacks, democracy becomes weaker. These women are not just part of democracy; they are helping it survive and grow by making it more fair and open.

**What's next step** – The future depends on whether governments and institutions support these women with protection and resources. If they do, democracy can become stronger and more inclusive. If not, shrinking spaces for women’s voices could lead to further democratic decline.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Women’s leadership is essential for keeping democracy strong and fair, especially when challenges try to silence their voices.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le leadership des femmes renforce la démocratie face aux défis croissants
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans le monde entier, la démocratie est menacée. Dans de nombreux pays, des lois et des pressions sociales rendent plus difficile la participation des citoyens, en particulier des femmes, à la vie publique et aux décisions.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des femmes leaders et militantes féministes se mobilisent pour protéger la démocratie. En Tanzanie, au Cameroun et au Brésil, elles surveillent les élections, réforment les médias et combattent les idées nuisibles sur le genre. Malgré les risques comme le harcèlement et les poursuites, elles poursuivent leur combat pour une participation juste.

**Impact** – C’est important car la démocratie repose sur l’égalité de parole et d’action. Quand les femmes sont exclues par des règles injustes, la violence ou les attaques en ligne, la démocratie s’affaiblit. Ces femmes ne sont pas seulement actrices de la démocratie, elles la font survivre et progresser en la rendant plus juste et ouverte.

**Prochaine étape** – L’avenir dépendra du soutien des gouvernements et institutions, qui doivent offrir protection et ressources. S’ils le font, la démocratie deviendra plus forte et inclusive. Sinon, la réduction des espaces pour les voix féminines pourrait aggraver le déclin démocratique.

**En une phrase** – Le leadership des femmes est essentiel pour maintenir une démocratie forte et équitable, surtout face aux tentatives de faire taire leurs voix.
articles.personality_title_es: El liderazgo de las mujeres fortalece la democracia ante crecientes desafíos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En todo el mundo, la democracia enfrenta graves amenazas. En muchos países, leyes y presiones sociales dificultan que las personas, especialmente las mujeres, participen en la vida pública y en la toma de decisiones.

**Qué pasó** – Mujeres líderes y activistas feministas están protegiendo la democracia. En países como Tanzania, Camerún y Brasil, supervisan elecciones, reforman medios de comunicación y luchan contra ideas dañinas sobre el género. A pesar de enfrentar riesgos como acoso y problemas legales, continúan trabajando para que todos puedan participar de manera justa.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque la democracia depende de que las personas tengan las mismas oportunidades para expresarse y participar. Cuando las mujeres son excluidas por reglas injustas, violencia o ataques en línea, la democracia se debilita. Estas mujeres no solo forman parte de la democracia, sino que la mantienen viva y la hacen crecer haciéndola más justa y abierta.

**Próximo paso** – El futuro depende de si los gobiernos e instituciones apoyan a estas mujeres con protección y recursos. Si lo hacen, la democracia puede fortalecerse y volverse más inclusiva. Si no, la reducción de espacios para las voces femeninas podría causar un mayor declive democrático.

**Una frase clave** – El liderazgo de las mujeres es fundamental para mantener la democracia fuerte y justa, especialmente cuando intentan silenciar sus voces.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a diverse group of intertwined, glowing hands forming a protective circle around a vibrant, stylized ballot box and a vintage microphone, set against a softly textured background of digital code streams and abstract media waves, using natural earth tones and muted blues to symbolize feminist leadership safeguarding democracy across physical, media, and digital spaces.

articles.title: Menopause at work: Germany starts breaking the taboo
articles.summary: While Germany faces a persistent shortage of skilled workers, one factor remains largely ignored: the impact of perimenopause. At this pivotal stage of their careers, many experienced women reduce their hours or leave the workforce because their needs are rarely recognised or supported. According to a survey, one in 10 women has left – or plans to leave – work due to menopause symptoms, while one in four reduces her working hours.
articles.content:
Menopause at work: Germany starts breaking the taboo

Europe

To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement. Accept Manage my choices One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site. Try again FOCUS © FRANCE 24 05:04

05:04 min Share

From the show

Reading time 1 min

While Germany faces a persistent shortage of skilled workers, one factor remains largely ignored: the impact of perimenopause. At this pivotal stage of their careers, many experienced women reduce their hours or leave the workforce because their needs are rarely recognised or supported. According to a survey, one in 10 women has left – or plans to leave – work due to menopause symptoms, while one in four reduces her working hours.

Fatigue, sleep problems and difficulty concentrating can directly affect productivity, leading to sick leave or job changes. The cost to the German economy is estimated at up to €9.4 billion a year. Now, faced with labour shortages, more companies are beginning to adapt – helping to break one of the last taboos affecting women at work. Our Berlin correspondents report.
articles.source: Health: news, videos, reports and analysis - France 24
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant social issue—menopause at work—that affects a large portion of the workforce and economy. It reports on Germany beginning to break the taboo and adapt workplaces to support women experiencing menopause, which can improve quality of life and workforce retention for many women. The story has broad societal impact and sufficient detail about the problem and emerging solutions.
articles.personality_title: Germany begins supporting women through menopause at work
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Germany is facing a shortage of skilled workers, but one important issue has been overlooked: how menopause affects women in the workplace. Many women in their middle years experience symptoms that make working full-time difficult.

**What happened** – A recent survey found that 1 in 10 women in Germany has left or plans to leave their job because of menopause symptoms like fatigue and trouble concentrating. Another 1 in 4 women reduces their working hours. These symptoms can cause sick leave or job changes.

**Impact** – Menopause-related work challenges cost the German economy up to €9.4 billion a year. Recognizing this, some companies are starting to change workplace policies to support women during menopause. This shift helps keep experienced women working and breaks a long-standing silence about menopause at work.

**What's next step** – As more companies adopt supportive measures, such as flexible hours or health programs, this could lead to better quality of life for women and reduce the loss of skilled workers. It may also encourage other countries to address menopause in the workplace.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Germany is beginning to recognize and support women experiencing menopause at work, helping them stay employed and improving the economy.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'Allemagne commence à soutenir les femmes en ménopause au travail
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'Allemagne fait face à une pénurie de travailleurs qualifiés, mais un problème important a été négligé : l'impact de la ménopause sur les femmes au travail. Beaucoup de femmes d'âge moyen vivent des symptômes qui rendent le travail à temps plein difficile.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un sondage récent montre qu'une femme sur dix en Allemagne a quitté ou prévoit de quitter son emploi à cause des symptômes de la ménopause, comme la fatigue et des difficultés de concentration. Une femme sur quatre réduit aussi ses heures de travail. Ces symptômes peuvent entraîner des arrêts maladie ou des changements d'emploi.

**Impact** – Les difficultés liées à la ménopause au travail coûtent jusqu'à 9,4 milliards d'euros par an à l'économie allemande. Pour y remédier, certaines entreprises commencent à adapter leurs politiques pour soutenir les femmes en ménopause. Ce changement aide à garder les femmes expérimentées en emploi et brise un tabou longtemps ignoré.

**Prochaine étape** – À mesure que plus d'entreprises mettent en place des mesures comme des horaires flexibles ou des programmes de santé, cela pourrait améliorer la qualité de vie des femmes et réduire la perte de travailleurs qualifiés. Cela pourrait aussi encourager d'autres pays à aborder la ménopause au travail.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L'Allemagne commence à reconnaître et soutenir les femmes en ménopause au travail, favorisant leur maintien dans l'emploi et améliorant l'économie.
articles.personality_title_es: Alemania comienza a apoyar a las mujeres durante la menopausia en el trabajo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Alemania enfrenta una escasez de trabajadores calificados, pero un problema importante ha sido ignorado: cómo la menopausia afecta a las mujeres en el lugar de trabajo. Muchas mujeres en edad media experimentan síntomas que dificultan trabajar a tiempo completo.

**Qué pasó** – Una encuesta reciente encontró que 1 de cada 10 mujeres en Alemania ha dejado o planea dejar su trabajo debido a síntomas de la menopausia, como fatiga y dificultad para concentrarse. Otra de cada 4 mujeres reduce sus horas de trabajo. Estos síntomas pueden causar bajas por enfermedad o cambios de empleo.

**Impacto** – Los problemas relacionados con la menopausia en el trabajo cuestan hasta 9,4 mil millones de euros al año a la economía alemana. Reconociendo esto, algunas empresas están comenzando a cambiar sus políticas para apoyar a las mujeres durante la menopausia. Este cambio ayuda a mantener a mujeres experimentadas trabajando y rompe un tabú de larga data.

**Próximo paso** – A medida que más empresas adopten medidas de apoyo, como horarios flexibles o programas de salud, esto podría mejorar la calidad de vida de las mujeres y reducir la pérdida de trabajadores calificados. También podría motivar a otros países a abordar la menopausia en el trabajo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Alemania empieza a reconocer y apoyar a las mujeres que pasan por la menopausia en el trabajo, ayudándolas a seguir empleadas y mejorando la economía.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy, open doorway symbolizing workplace inclusion, framed by soft glowing light that gently illuminates a subtle gradient of shifting colors representing the stages of perimenopause, with abstract silhouettes of diverse women in thoughtful, supportive postures around the threshold, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft pastels to evoke understanding and empowerment.
